learning_outcomes.description: Identify and critically evaluate the assumptions that drive debates on contemporary political issues.
Contextualise contemporary political debates in relation to relevant theories of political science.
Situate contemporary political debates in the relevant histories of public policy.
Apply the necessary tools and skills for critical analysis in reading, textual analysis and writing.
Collaborate in groups, to speak and write persuasively and in an evidence-based manner about a range of political issues.

assessments.due_date: This item is due at the end of each class for Weeks 1-7 and 9-11. Time will be allocated for students to submit their work.
assessments.task_description:
This course has ten weeks where in-class group activities (with 3-4 students in each group) will be conducted (Weeks 1-7; 9-11).  

Students will be given time to submit their group's activity at the end of the class.  

These will be graded out of 3 with each member of the group awarded the same grade.  Your top 5 highest individual grades will count towards the 15% mark. If you fail to complete five, you will be marked accordingly.   

If problematic group work is observed, individual marks will be awarded for that week.

Marking rubrics are available on the Blackboard site and discussed in Week One. 

This task is completed in person and has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance.

A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Please include each group member's name on the submission. Each student must upload their submission to Blackboard.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.due_date:
14/09/2026 - 18/09/2026

Presentations will occur in Week 8, either in class or by appointment, depending on class size.
assessments.task_description:
Your group will be required to provide a ten-minute in-person Ministerial-level Briefing in Week 8.  

In your group, you will identify a key issue in Australian state or federal politics where gender inequality is a significant theme.  This may be an existing policy issue (such as childcare subsidies), it may be an emerging political issue (for example need to change community’s views on family and domestic violence), or it may be an issue relevant to a political party (such as an argument for gender quotas in a political party).  Your group must: 

  • Form groups of between 3-5 students, in Week 4 
  • Determine who the briefing is for (eg: a relevant federal/state minister, the Premier, the Opposition Leader, etc) 
  • Clearly explain the issue, its urgency, who is impacted by this and in what way. Include relevant data where appropriate 
  • Provide a gender-responsive analysis of the issue 
  • Provide advice to the authority.  You might wish to consider:  
  • What the policy response should be 
  • What the strengths, weaknesses, constraints and opportunities are in addressing the issue in this way 
  • What the timeline and key milestones are for this plan 
  • What the measures of success are 

Your presentation should be supported by powerpoint.  Your group should then be prepared to answer 5 minutes of questions.  You will be assessed as a group on your presentation and your response to questions. You will also receive an individual mark based on peer-assessment of your contribution to the group. 

Students will be asked to form groups in the tutorial in week 4, complete a peer-assessment check-in (via Buddycheck) in Week 6, and a final peer-assessment review (via Buddycheck) by Friday 5pm of Week 8, after the presentations. This final peer assessment will count towards an individual grade component of the task. Failure to participate in the peer-assessment will result in a mark of 0 for the individual peer-marked component of the task.  

What you need to submit: 

  • Group Powerpoint slide submitted by Monday 1pm of Week 8 
  • Peer-assessment (Buddycheck) by Friday 5pm Week 6 
  • Peer-assessment (Buddycheck) by Friday 5pm, Week 8 (after the presentation) 

 Marking rubrics and percentage weightings are available on the Blackboard site and discussed in Week One. 

This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance.

A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Presentations will be video recorded but not shared beyond the teaching team. Please submit your powerpoint via Blackboard by Monday 14 September at 1pm.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.due_date:
27/07/2026 - 12/10/2026

You may submit this assessment at any time during the semester after the relevant activity has taken place. Submissions will be returned within two weeks. The last submission date in Monday 12 October 1pm (Week 11).
assessments.task_description:
Choose one of the activities undertaken in class during the course.  Write a 1,000 word applied paper on the activity.  The structure of this submission is flexible and can be shaped by the activity.   

You must: 

  • Include a photo/attachment of the final output of the group for the relevant task (not counted in word count) 
  • Provide a mini literature review on this topic, both scholarly and, if appropriate, policy/ media/ speeches, etc (approx 200-300 words) 
  • Include a reference list (not counted in word count). 
  • Expand on the original in-class task if needed 
  • Reflect on the approach, debates, and reasoning of the group – and any points at which you personally deviated from the group’s decision-making (300-400 words) 
  • Identify the likely strengths, weaknesses, gaps, barriers, constraints or risks of your group’s approach (as appropriate) (200-300 words) 

Marking rubrics are available on the Blackboard site and discussed in Week One. 

This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance.

A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Assignments for this course will be submitted electronically via Blackboard and using Turnitin.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
The final assessment for this course will be a central exam held during the central exam period. Students will have 2 hours to complete the exam.  

Students will be required to answer 2 questions in essay form. Students will be required to complete one question from Part A of the exam questions and the other from Part B. The topics covered in Part A correspond approximately with the first half of the course, while the topics covered in Part B correspond with the lectures in the second half of the course. Students will choose one of 6 possible questions from Part A and one of 6 possible questions from Part B. 

Each of the two questions will be worth 15 marks. The maximum score for the assessment will be 30 marks. 

Marking rubrics are available on the Blackboard site and discussed in Week One. 

This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.
